risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization was 2:1 everolimus versus matching placebo, using the SciRan (Scientific Randomization) program developed at BCH. Randomization was stratified by age and IQ with two levels for each factor." Comment: This describes a computerized randomization program and stratified randomization, which are context clues indicating a proper random allocation sequence was likely used.

Summary: The paper states that participants were randomized using the SciRan program and that the study was double-blind, but it gives no explicit information about how the allocation sequence was concealed from the person enrolling participants. Comment: Computer-generated randomization and blinding do not by themselves demonstrate concealment; there is no mention of central allocation, SNOSE, identical numbered containers, or any other mechanism preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a prospective, double-blind randomized, placebo-controlled two-center phase II study." and "All study staff were blinded to randomization assignment, except for one physician at BCH to whom treatment assignment and serum trough levels were available to make protocol-defined dose-adjustment recommendations... The unblinded physician otherwise had no direct access to participants, clinical data, or assessment results." Comment: Participants received matching placebo and study staff were blinded, with only a limited unblinded physician role that had no direct participant contact or access to outcomes. Because the main outcomes included neurocognitive/behavioral assessments and adverse events, which could plausibly be influenced by awareness of allocation, the reported successful blinding supports a low risk judgement overall.

Quote: "All study staff were blinded to randomization assignment, except for one physician at BCH to whom treatment assignment and serum trough levels were available to make protocol-defined dose-adjustment recommendations... The unblinded physician otherwise had no direct access to participants, clinical data, or assessment results." Comment: Outcome assessment appears to have been conducted by blinded study staff, and the one unblinded physician did not access outcome data. The main outcomes were largely direct neuropsychological testing by study neuropsychologists/psychometrists, computer-based tasks, and standardized parent/caregiver rating scales. While some outcomes are patient-/caregiver-reported, the participants and assessment staff were blinded within a double-blind placebo-controlled design, so knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence measurement.

Quote: "Forty‐two of the 47 randomized (89%) completed the study." and "The study was designed to... Assuming a dropout rate of 10%..." Comment: Attrition was modest overall and appears acceptable for a 6-month RCT; by 6 months, 13/15 placebo and 29/32 everolimus participants had outcome data, with no signal of marked imbalance in dropout or reasons clearly related to lack of efficacy. The analysis also used a linear mixed-effects model and explicitly stated that missing observations were assumed missing at random, which is a plausible approach for this level of missingness. This makes attrition bias unlikely.
